mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Germany on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe. Germany sits right in the middle of Europe, with the North Sea above it and the Alps below 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Germany?
mapQuestions.answer: Berlin, in the north-east of the country. Until 1989 it was split in half by a wall between communist East Germany and capitalist West Germany.

mapQuestions.question: How many countries border Germany?
mapQuestions.answer: Nine: Denmark, Poland, Czech Republic, Austria, Switzerland, France, Luxembourg, Belgium and the Netherlands. More than any other European country except Russia.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Germany?
mapQuestions.answer: The Zugspitze, 2,962 metres, on the border with Austria in the Bavarian Alps.

mapQuestions.question: How long is the Rhine river through Germany?
mapQuestions.answer: About 865 km. The Rhine is one of Europe's most important rivers and runs through (or borders) six countries before reaching the North Sea.

foods.name: Bratwurst
foods.description: A grilled pork sausage, served in a small bread roll with mustard. Almost every German town has its own kind, with its own seasoning, going back centuries.

foods.name: Pretzel (Brezel)
foods.description: A salt-crusted twisted bread, soft inside and chewy outside. The Bavarian version is huge and pillowy, eaten warm with mustard or butter.

foods.name: Schnitzel
foods.description: A thin slice of pork or veal, breaded and pan-fried until golden. Usually served with potato salad, lemon and parsley. Wiener Schnitzel is the most famous version (technically Austrian).

foods.name: Sauerkraut
foods.description: Cabbage finely shredded and left to ferment for weeks until it turns sour. Sounds strange, tastes amazing with sausages. Full of probiotics, so good for your gut.

foods.name: Black Forest cake
foods.description: Layers of chocolate sponge soaked in cherry brandy, layered with whipped cream and dark cherries, with chocolate shavings on top. From the Black Forest region in the south-west.

foods.name: Currywurst
foods.description: A sliced pork sausage smothered in spiced tomato-curry sauce, dusted with curry powder, served with chips or a bread roll. Berlin street-food classic since the 1940s.

scavengerItems.label: A real pretzel hanging in a bakery window
A German-brand car (BMW, Mercedes, VW, Audi, Porsche)
A castle on a hilltop, on a river bend, or in a forest
A real "Fachwerk" half-timbered house (black wooden beams on white walls)
A spotless bicycle path running beside a road
A Christmas market stall, or any winter market sign
A clock tower (Rathaus or Glockenspiel) chiming the hour
A beer garden under chestnut trees
A "Vorsicht" warning sign or a clear, crisp Deutsche Bahn timetable
A piece of the Berlin Wall in a museum or on a street
Someone in Dirndl or Lederhosen (Oktoberfest / village fest)
A Trabant — the boxy East German car still occasionally on the road in Berlin

geoMatches.description: The capital. East and West for 28 years, reunited in 1990. Edgy, gritty, alternative, packed with museums and history. Cheaper than most German cities.
The Bavarian capital. Beer halls, lederhosen, Oktoberfest in autumn, white sausages for breakfast, and the gateway to the Alps.
A Rhine river city with a gigantic Gothic cathedral that took 632 years to build. The cathedral somehow survived WW2 bombing, even when everything around it was destroyed.
The big port city in the north. Maze of canals, a red-brick warehouse district, the Beatles' first stage, and a fish market on Sunday mornings.
A dense range of mountain forests in the south-west. Cuckoo clocks, dark fir trees, deep lakes, fairy tale villages, and the famous Black Forest cake.
A 19th-century fairytale castle in the Bavarian Alps. Built by an eccentric king who lived alone in it. The inspiration for Disney's Sleeping Beauty castle.

senses.smell: Bratwurst grilling, fresh-baked pretzels, beer halls, pine forests, glühwein (mulled wine) at a Christmas market, roasted almonds, wood smoke in the Black Forest…
senses.hear: Church bells across an old town, "Hallo", "Danke", oompah brass bands, beer-hall singing, a crisp Deutsche Bahn train announcement, bicycle bells in every city…
senses.taste: Sharp mustard on a bratwurst, salty soft pretzel, curry-dusted currywurst sauce, sweet apfelstrudel with vanilla sauce, hot Glühwein in winter, tangy sauerkraut, dark Black Forest cherries…
senses.feel: Cold mountain air in winter, the polished wood of a long beer-hall bench, cobblestones underfoot, soft new snow, sticky strudel pastry on your fingers…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Germany so obsessed with Christmas markets?
stories.body: Germany invented Christmas markets in the 1400s. Every town and city across the country has its own from late November right up to Christmas Eve. Wooden stalls sell hot mulled wine (Glühwein) in collectable mugs, gingerbread (Lebkuchen), bratwurst, hand-carved wooden ornaments and roasted almonds. Some markets are over 600 years old. The biggest is the Christkindlesmarkt in Nürnberg. Streets twinkle with lights, choirs sing carols, kids ride wooden carousels and ice skate on flooded squares. For the whole month of December the country smells of cinnamon, cloves and roasting almonds.

stories.question: Why does Germany have 20,000 castles?
stories.body: Germany has around 20,000 castles, more than any other country on Earth. Many were built in the Middle Ages when local lords needed fortified bases to defend their land — Germany was not one country back then but hundreds of small kingdoms and principalities. The most famous fairytale castle is Neuschwanstein in the Bavarian Alps, built in the 1800s by King Ludwig II (the "Mad King" or "Swan King") who lived in it almost completely alone. Walt Disney saw photos of Neuschwanstein and used it as the model for the Sleeping Beauty Castle at Disneyland. Many German castles are now open to visit, and most German kids tour at least one a year on school trips.

stories.question: Who invented the modern car?
stories.body: In 1885, Karl Benz built the first practical motor car in Mannheim, Germany. The next summer, his wife Bertha, fed up with the slow pace of his demonstrations, secretly took the car at dawn and drove 105 km to visit her mother in Pforzheim, becoming the first long-distance driver in history. Along the way she invented brake pads (she had a cobbler add leather to the brakes when they wore out) and refuelled at a pharmacy (they sold the petrol-like cleaning fluid she needed). Germany now makes some of the world's most famous car brands — VW, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Porsche, Audi. The autobahn, the German motorway system, still has stretches with no speed limit at all.

stories.question: What was the Berlin Wall?
stories.body: After World War 2 ended in 1945, Germany was split into two countries by the winners: communist East Germany (controlled by the Soviet Union) and capitalist West Germany. Berlin sat inside East Germany but was itself split in two. On the night of 13 August 1961, East Germany suddenly built a 155 km wall around West Berlin, to stop its own citizens fleeing west. Families were separated for 28 years. Around 140 people died trying to cross. On 9 November 1989, after weeks of protest, the East German government suddenly opened the gates. Strangers from both sides hugged on top of the wall, then chipped it down with hammers. Pieces of the wall are now in museums all over the world. Germany has spent the last 35 years putting itself back together as one country.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A wall fell because ordinary people kept showing up to protest, week after week. What change would you keep protesting for, year after year?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best German food you have tried so far?
Have you heard a brass band, an oompah tune, or a Bavarian song?
Punctuality is sacred here. What's been the most "German" thing about how things run?
What's been the biggest castle, cathedral or town hall you have seen?
Bikes are everywhere in some cities. Have you ridden one yet?
Germany teaches its Nazi-era history in every school. Should every country teach its worst moments that openly?

wordSearch.hint: German foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: Every train station, every market square, every beer-garden floor.

animals.name: Hooded crow
animals.description: Grey-and-black crow on the rooftops and parks, especially in the east.

animals.name: Red fox
animals.description: In Berlin you might see one trotting down a city street at 4am.

animals.name: Roe deer
animals.description: Small reddish-brown deer in fields at the forest edge, often at dusk.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Common in German forests and surprisingly bold around suburbs.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Returning to chimney-top nests every spring after wintering in Africa.
